summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that engineers are terrible at salary negotiation and have turned this incompetence into a misguided badge of honor. He frames negotiation as a brief, high-leverage activity worth potentially $100,000+ over a career, using the analogy of reading poetry while standing on one foot for $100,000. The post systematically dismantles psychological barriers to negotiation, explains how employers actually think about compensation (in terms of fully-loaded costs where $5,000 is trivial), and provides tactical advice including never giving a number first, using the employer's own language, and trading across multiple dimensions of compensation. McKenzie emphasizes that negotiation should only happen after receiving a 'Yes-If' — agreement in principle that you'll be hired if terms are reached — and that the process is far less adversarial than most candidates imagine.
key_points: A $5,000-$15,000 salary increase compounds over a decade into six figures when factoring in raises, 401k contributions, and future salary anchoring
Employers think in fully-loaded costs (150-200% of salary), making small salary differences trivial to them but life-changing to you — the asymmetry is enormous
Never give a number first; every request for your salary history or requirements is an attempt to anchor you to an anomalously low number
Only negotiate after receiving a 'Yes-If' — the company has already invested thousands in interviewing you and desperately wants the deal to close
Salary is just one dimension; vacation days, project assignments, professional development, and other levers can be traded when salary hits a ceiling
money_quotes: We overwhelmingly suck at it. We have turned sucking at it into a perverse badge of virtue.
You can trivially pick up $5,000 in salary negotiations just by sucking less.
Virtually any amount of money available to you personally is mouse droppings to your prospective employer.
The absolute worst outcome of negotiating an offer in good faith is that you will get exactly the contents of that offer.
Volunteering a number when asked says the same thing to people with hiring authority that flunking FizzBuzz says to an engineer.
Everyone in this discussion is a businessman. Some might call themselves 'regular employees,' which just means they're businessmen with self-confidence issues and poor business skills.
Bob is not spending Bob's money to hire you. Bob is spending Bob's budget.
Resumes are an institution created to mean that no one has to read resumes.
key_insight: Salary negotiation is a brief, high-leverage skill that engineers irrationally avoid, yet the asymmetry between what $5,000 means to you versus your employer means even modest negotiation effort yields outsized career-long financial returns.
